data.title: Stripe's ambition: to build the financial infrastructure for the era of AI and stablecoins.
data.description:
Tao Zhu, Jinse Finance

Summary: Stripe has long been considered one of the world's largest internet payment companies. Recently, Stripe's development direction has changed significantly. From acquiring Bridge and Privy, to rumors of acquiring OpenRouter and PayPal, and jointly promoting Open USD, Stripe is evolving from a payment company into a global financial empire spanning payments, stablecoins, wallets, and AI. What exactly does Stripe want?

I. Negotiating the Acquisition of OpenRouter—Entering the AI ​​Market

Recently, Stripe has made frequent new strategic moves, launching a series of initiatives.

According to the Wall Street Journal, citing sources familiar with the matter, Stripe is in talks to acquire AI model aggregation platform OpenRouter, and the deal could be finalized soon. While negotiations are not yet finalized, the potential transaction amount is approximately $10 billion, while OpenRouter's valuation after its latest funding round in May was approximately $1.3 billion. This means that if the acquisition is successful, its valuation will jump nearly eightfold in a short period.

If this acquisition is successful, it will signify Stripe's official expansion from the payment infrastructure sector into AI infrastructure. Unlike large AI models like OpenAI and Anthropic, OpenRouter itself does not train large models; instead, it acts as a model switch for the AI ​​era. Currently, OpenRouter has aggregated hundreds of large language models, including those from OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, Meta, and numerous open-source models. Developers only need to access a single API provided by OpenRouter to freely switch between different models without needing to interface with each model vendor separately. According to Stripe's official website, the two companies reached a partnership as early as the end of January this year: the OpenRouter platform has already utilized Stripe to support its revenue growth, global expansion, and fraud prevention. Stripe's collaboration with OpenRouter allows developers to route model requests through OpenRouter, while Stripe automatically tracks usage, applies appropriate pricing, and handles billing. The collaboration between Stripe and OpenRouter enables developers to automatically respond to cost changes and scale profitability without increasing operational complexity. Additionally, it's worth noting the founder of the OpenRouter platform—Alex Atallah. Alex Atallah co-founded the NFT trading platform OpenSea with Devin Finzer in 2017, serving as its CTO. During the NFT bull market, OpenSea grew into the world's largest NFT trading platform, with monthly transaction volume exceeding $4 billion between 2017 and 2022, making it one of the most successful startups in Web3 history. In 2022, Alex Atallah left OpenSea's management but retained a board seat, subsequently founding OpenRouter in 2023, officially shifting its focus to the AI ​​infrastructure sector. II. Plans to Jointly Acquire PayPal—Completing the Payment Landscape As early as the end of February this year, Stripe expressed initial interest in acquiring all or part of PayPal's business, but a deal has yet to be reached. On July 15, according to two sources familiar with the matter, payment company Stripe and private equity firm Advent International jointly offered to acquire PayPal (PYPL.O) for $60.50 per share, a deal that would value the payment company at over $53 billion. The sources said the offer, submitted earlier this month, has secured approximately $50 billion in committed financing from banks, representing a premium of about 28% over PayPal's closing price on Tuesday. Another source revealed that PayPal's board believes the $53 billion acquisition offer from rival Stripe and Advent International undervalues ​​the company and faces regulatory and financing difficulties. Stripe is a payment infrastructure provider, while PayPal is more consumer-oriented. As of 2026, PayPal has over 400 million active accounts and, in addition to the PayPal Wallet, owns Venmo, one of the largest mobile payment platforms in the US, and covers merchant payments, cross-border remittances, and enterprise payments through businesses such as Braintree and Xoom. What truly attracted Stripe may be PayPal's continued investment in the crypto ecosystem in recent years. In 2023, PayPal officially launched its US dollar stablecoin, PYUSD, becoming the world's first major listed payment company to issue a US dollar stablecoin. PYUSD is issued by Paxos and regulated by the New York State Department of Financial Services. It is deployed on the Ethereum and Solana networks and is gradually expanding into cross-border payments, merchant settlements, and on-chain finance. III. Acquisition of Bridge – Seizing Stablecoin Infrastructure In February 2025, Stripe announced the formal completion of its acquisition of stablecoin infrastructure company Bridge for approximately $1.1 billion. Founded in 2022 by two former Coinbase executives, Zach Abrams and Sean Yu, Bridge positions itself as a stablecoin financial infrastructure for enterprises. According to its official website, Bridge describes itself as an "end-to-end stablecoin platform," providing enterprises with capabilities such as stablecoin issuance, custody, cross-border settlement, fiat currency exchange, and fund management; it can be seen as a stablecoin version of Stripe. For Stripe, Bridge's greatest value lies in completing the underlying infrastructure for stablecoins. Stripe has shifted its focus from primarily handling payment interfaces and fund flows to providing stablecoin account management, cross-border settlement, fiat currency exchange, and global fund transfer capabilities.

IV. Acquisition of Privy – Bringing Wallets Under Its Control

In June 2025, Stripe acquired Privy, a cryptocurrency wallet infrastructure company.

Founded in 2021, Privy primarily provides embedded wallet solutions for developers. Through Privy, developers can create wallets for users directly within their applications. Users don't even need to save mnemonic phrases or install browser plugins; they can register using only an email address, phone number, or social media account, significantly lowering the barrier to entry for Web3 products.

For Stripe, this acquisition signifies its formal expansion from payments to the cryptocurrency wallet field.

V. Jointly Promoting OpenUSD – Competing for Stablecoin Standard Setting Rights

In addition to continuous acquisitions, Stripe has also begun participating in the development of underlying standards for stablecoins.

In late June of this year, Open Standard launched Open USD (OUSD), with over 140 initial partners. These partners encompass payment and financial services (Stripe, Visa, Mastercard, American Express), asset management (BlackRock), banks (BNY, DBS, Standard Chartered, BBVA), crypto-native institutions (Coinbase, Aave, MetaMask, Morpho, Solana), and technology and retail platforms (Google, Shopify, DoorDash). OUSD returns most of its reserve profits to participating partners, minting a small management fee. Enterprise minting and redemption of OUSD are completely free, with no supply cap. Essentially, this distributes the profits earned by Tether and Circle to alliance members. Interestingly, Open Standard's interim CEO is Zach Abrams, who is also the co-founder of the stablecoin startup Bridge, which, as mentioned above, was acquired by Stripe for $1.1 billion in 2025.

VI. What Does Stripe Really Want?

Stripe has always been known for its payments business, but now it's attempting a different path—becoming the financial infrastructure for the AI ​​and crypto era.

Specifically, if both pending acquisitions ultimately succeed, then: OpenRouter will be responsible for AI models; Bridge will handle stablecoin clearing; OpenUSD will drive stablecoin standards; Privy will provide digital identity and wallets; and PayPal will enhance its consumer payment network and global user access.

Stripe's competitors have expanded from traditional payment giants like PayPal to a wider range of areas: in payments, it will face Visa and Mastercard; in stablecoins, it will compete with Circle and Tether; in AI infrastructure, it is beginning to intersect with the developer market covered by AI ecosystems such as OpenAI and Anthropic; in digital wallets, it is vying with MetaMask… These seemingly fragmented businesses together constitute a complete financial infrastructure landscape.

This is why the capital market's valuation of Stripe has multiplied.

In 2014, just four years after its founding, Stripe completed its Series B funding round, valuing the company at approximately $1.75 billion. In 2018, its valuation surpassed $20 billion. In March 2021, Stripe completed another round of funding, with its valuation soaring to $95 billion. This year, Stripe's latest employee share buyback has further increased its valuation to approximately $159 billion. The capital market is revaluing Stripe not only because it remains a leader in the payments industry, but also because it is developing several currently hot and promising markets, including stablecoin infrastructure and an AI developer ecosystem. In summary, its former rival PayPal propelled the widespread adoption of internet payments. Now, Stripe aims not only to acquire its former competitor but also to become the financial infrastructure for the AI ​​and stablecoin era. From payments to stablecoins, crypto wallets, and AI… Stripe seems to have no business boundaries.

data.title: The Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate have revised the judicial interpretations on criminal cases involving insider trading and disclosure of inside information.
data.description:
Punishing Insider Trading and Insider Information Disclosure Crimes According to Law, Strengthening the Rule of Law in the Capital Market, and Safeguarding the Legitimate Rights and Interests of Investors. — The Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate jointly issued the "Decision on Amending the Interpretation of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Several Issues Concerning the Specific Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Insider Trading and Insider Information Disclosure" (Fa Shi [2026] No. 13, hereinafter referred to as the "Amendment Decision") on July 24, 2026. The Amendment Decision was adopted at the 1961st meeting of the Judicial Committee of the Supreme People's Court and the 75th meeting of the 14th Procuratorial Committee of the Supreme People's Procuratorate, and will come into effect on July 27, 2026. Since its implementation on June 1, 2012, the Interpretation on Several Issues Concerning the Specific Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Insider Trading and Disclosure of Inside Information (hereinafter referred to as the Interpretation) has played a positive role in punishing crimes of insider trading and disclosure of inside information. In recent years, new situations and changes have emerged in crimes of insider trading and disclosure of inside information, and relevant laws in the securities and futures fields have also been amended. To ensure the accurate, unified, and effective implementation of the law and to more precisely and forcefully punish related crimes, the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate conducted in-depth investigations and research, widely solicited opinions, and repeatedly discussed and improved the draft, resulting in the Amendment Decision. The Amendment Decision adheres to the guidance of Xi Jinping Thought on Socialism with Chinese Characteristics for a New Era, thoroughly implements the spirit of the 20th National Congress of the Communist Party of China and its subsequent plenary sessions, upholds the principle of severely punishing insider trading and insider information disclosure crimes according to law, contributes to the rule of law in the capital market, actively creates an open, fair, and just market environment, and effectively safeguards the legitimate rights and interests of investors. Firstly, it further clarifies the stricter legal responsibilities of key entities. A new clause is added to Article 5 of the Interpretation, stipulating that "the time when a controlling shareholder, actual controller, or relevant decision-maker discloses the preliminary intention to form insider information to a closely related person, or the time when a related transaction is conducted based on that preliminary intention, shall be deemed the initial time of the motion." The first part of the document clarifies and specifies the sensitive period for insider information involving key entities that play a crucial role in generating insider information, preventing them from exploiting time differences for insider trading or leaking insider information, further highlighting key areas for punishment, and strengthening source governance. The second part further improves the grounds for excluding insider trading. The relevant provisions of Article 4 of the "Interpretation" are revised, emphasizing that acquisitions of listed companies must conform to the acquisition purpose, preventing acquisition personnel from engaging in insider trading using "hidden accounts"; emphasizing that pre-arranged transactions must be genuine and legal, preventing perpetrators from using false or illegal contracts, instructions, or plans to evade legal sanctions; clarifying the public nature of "disclosure" in item 3 of this article, fairly protecting the legitimate rights and interests of all types of investors, adhering to strict control over defenses that do not constitute insider trading, and continuously strengthening the legal network. The third part ensures the consistency of the regulatory system. The Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate have revised the names, articles, and relevant content of the laws cited in Articles 1 and 5 of the Interpretation to align with the relevant provisions of the newly revised Securities Law and the newly promulgated Futures and Derivatives Law. Articles 6 and 7 of the Interpretation have also been revised to coordinate with the standards for filing and prosecuting cases stipulated in Article 30 of the "Provisions on the Standards for Filing and Prosecuting Criminal Cases under the Jurisdiction of Public Security Organs (II)" issued by the Supreme People's Procuratorate and the Ministry of Public Security on April 6, 2022, promoting a reasonable connection between criminal and administrative prosecution and building a comprehensive accountability system. Next, the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate will guide local people's courts and people's procuratorates at all levels to thoroughly implement Xi Jinping's economic thought and Xi Jinping's rule of law thought, accurately understand and apply the Amendment Decision, and severely punish insider trading and insider information disclosure crimes in accordance with the law, providing stronger judicial guarantees for the sound operation of the capital market. The Decision of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Amending the Interpretation of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Several Issues Concerning the Specific Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Insider Trading and Disclosure of Inside Information was adopted at the 1961st meeting of the Judicial Committee of the Supreme People's Court on December 13, 2025, and at the 75th meeting of the 14th Procuratorial Committee of the Supreme People's Procuratorate on April 17, 2026. It is hereby promulgated and shall come into effect on July 27, 2026. Supreme People's Court and Supreme People's Procuratorate Interpretation No. 13 of 2026 (July 24, 2026) The Decision on Several Issues Concerning the Interpretation of the Supreme People's Court's Judicial Committee on December 13, 2025, and the Supreme People's Procuratorate's 14th Procuratorial Committee on April 17, 2026, shall come into effect on July 27, 2026. Based on judicial practice, the following amendments are hereby made to the "Interpretations of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Several Issues Concerning the Specific Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Insider Trading and Disclosure of Inside Information" (Fa Shi [2012] No. 6): I. Article 1, Item 1 is amended to read: "Persons who are aware of inside information as stipulated in Article 51 of the Securities Law." Article 1, Item 2 is amended to read: "Persons who are aware of inside information related to futures trading as stipulated in Article 15 of the Futures and Derivatives Law." II. Article 4 is amended to read: "Anyone who falls under any of the following circumstances shall not be considered as engaging in securities or futures trading related to inside information as stipulated in Article 180, Paragraph 1 of the Criminal Law:" "(i) A natural person, legal person, or unincorporated organization that holds or jointly holds more than 5% of the shares of a listed company with others through agreements or other arrangements acquires shares of the listed company, except where the transaction is clearly inconsistent with the purpose of the acquisition and there is no justifiable reason;" (ii) Engaging in relevant securities or futures transactions in accordance with written contracts, instructions, or plans that have clearly defined transaction methods, quantities, prices, and times, entered into before the formation of inside information or before the knowledge or acquisition of inside information, and the relevant contracts, instructions, or plans comply with the provisions of the law;" (iii) Trading based on information that has been publicly disclosed by others;" “(iv) The transaction has other legitimate reasons or legitimate sources of information.” III. Article 5, Paragraph 2 is amended to read: “The time of occurrence of the ‘major events’ listed in Article 80, Paragraph 2 and Article 81, Paragraph 2 of the Securities Law, and the time of formation of the ‘policies’, ‘decisions’, etc., stipulated in Article 14, Paragraph 2 of the Futures and Derivatives Law shall be deemed as the time of formation of inside information.” A new paragraph, Paragraph 4, is added after Paragraph 3: “The time when the controlling shareholder, actual controller, or relevant decision-making personnel disclose the preliminary intention to form inside information to closely related persons, or the time when a related transaction is conducted based on such preliminary intention, shall be deemed as the initial time of the motion.” The fourth paragraph is amended to the fifth paragraph. IV. Article 6 is amended to read: "Anyone who, during the sensitive period of insider information, engages in or explicitly or implicitly suggests that others engage in or discloses insider information leading to others engaging in securities or futures trading related to that insider information, and falls under any of the following circumstances, shall be deemed to have committed 'serious circumstances' as stipulated in Article 180, Paragraph 1 of the Criminal Law: "(i) The transaction amount of securities trading exceeds two million yuan; "(ii) The amount of margin used in futures trading exceeds one million yuan; " “(iii) The amount of profit or loss avoided exceeds RMB 500,000;” “(iv) Engaging in insider trading or disclosing insider information three or more times within two years;” “(v) Explicitly or implicitly instructing three or more persons to engage in securities or futures trading related to insider information;” “(vi) Other serious circumstances.” "Any transaction involving a securities transaction amount exceeding one million yuan, or a futures transaction involving a margin deposit exceeding 500,000 yuan, or a profit or loss avoided exceeding 250,000 yuan, shall be deemed 'serious circumstances' as stipulated in Article 180, Paragraph 1 of the Criminal Law if any of the following circumstances exist: "(i) An insider who is aware of inside information as stipulated in the Securities Law, Futures and Derivatives Law, engages in or jointly engages in insider trading with others; "(ii) By selling or indirectly selling inside information, explicitly or implicitly instructing others to engage in transactions related to that inside information; "(iii) Having been criminally prosecuted for securities or futures crimes;" "(iv) Those who have been subject to administrative penalties for securities or futures violations within the past two years;" "(v) Those who have caused other serious consequences."" "V. Article 7 is amended as follows: "If, during the sensitive period of insider information, one engages in or explicitly or implicitly instructs others to engage in or discloses insider information leading others to engage in securities or futures trading related to that insider information, and one of the following circumstances exists, it shall be deemed as 'particularly serious circumstances' as stipulated in Article 180, Paragraph 1 of the Criminal Law:" "(i) The transaction amount of securities trading exceeds 20 million yuan;" "(ii) The amount of margin used for futures trading exceeds ten million yuan;" "(iii) The amount of profit or loss avoided exceeds five million yuan;" "(iv) Other particularly serious circumstances."" VI. Article 4 is moved to after Article 9, and Article 5 is moved to after Article 7, with corresponding adjustments to the article numbers. This decision shall take effect on July 27, 2026. According to this decision, the "Interpretations of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Several Issues Concerning the Specific Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Insider Trading and Disclosure of Inside Information" shall be amended accordingly and republished. The Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate issued an interpretation on several issues concerning the specific application of law in handling criminal cases involving insider trading and the disclosure of insider information. (Adopted at the 1529th meeting of the Judicial Committee of the Supreme People's Court on October 31, 2011, and the 72nd meeting of the 11th Procuratorial Committee of the Supreme People's Procuratorate on February 27, 2012; amended according to the "Decision of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Amending the 'Interpretations of the Supreme People's Court and the Supreme People's Procuratorate on Several Issues Concerning the Specific Application of Law in Handling Criminal Cases of Insider Trading and Disclosure of Inside Information'" adopted at the 1961st meeting of the Judicial Committee of the Supreme People's Court on December 13, 2025, and the 75th meeting of the 14th Procuratorial Committee of the Supreme People's Procuratorate on April 17, 2026, which shall take effect on July 27, 2026)

In order to maintain the order of securities and futures market management and punish securities and futures crimes according to law, in accordance with the relevant provisions of the Criminal Law, the following interpretations are made on several issues concerning the specific application of law in handling criminal cases of insider trading and disclosure of inside information:

data.title: BitMEX Sued Over Alleged Forced Liquidation Scheme as Exchange Prepares to Shut Down
data.description:
BitMEX Sued Over Bitcoin Losses Hours After Announcing Shutdown

Two BitMEX customers have taken the crypto derivatives exchange to court, accusing it of rigging its liquidation system to strip traders of their Bitcoin and pocket the difference.

The proposed class action, filed by BKX Services Inc. and trader David Namdar in the US District Court for the Southern District of New York, lands on the very day BitMEX confirmed it is shutting down for good after more than a decade in business.

What Are The Plaintiffs Claiming

According to the complaint, the two plaintiffs together lost 622.66 BTC (roughly $40.9 million) through forced liquidations on the platform.

BREAKING: BitMEX faces class-action lawsuit alleging engineered liquidations seized 622.66 BTC in customer collateral ahead of exchange shutdown. pic.twitter.com/avfgu1n14M

— MSB Intel (@MSBIntel) July 24, 2026

BKX says it lost at least 305.81 BTC (about $20.1 million), while Namdar puts his losses at more than 316.85 BTC (around $20.8 million).

The lawsuit alleges that BitMEX's liquidation engine was not just automated risk management. It was built to make money for the exchange.

🚨𝗕𝗜𝗧𝗠𝗘𝗫 𝗦𝗨𝗘𝗗 𝗜𝗡 𝗡𝗬 𝗙𝗘𝗗𝗘𝗥𝗔𝗟 𝗖𝗢𝗨𝗥𝗧 𝗢𝗩𝗘𝗥 𝗜𝗡𝗧𝗘𝗥𝗡𝗔𝗟 𝗧𝗥𝗔𝗗𝗜𝗡𝗚 𝗦𝗖𝗛𝗘𝗠𝗘!

BKX Services and David Namdar filed a lawsuit in the Southern District of New York accusing BitMEX of profiting from privileged trade access, server freezes, and… pic.twitter.com/pSua5YHWh2

— Rahul K (@iamrahulinc) July 24, 2026

Traders using leverage of up to 100 times their collateral were reportedly liquidated even when their remaining collateral was still worth far more than what they owed, with the leftover Bitcoin funnelled into BitMEX's insurance fund rather than returned to customers.

The plaintiffs alleged in the complaint,

"BitMEX deliberately developed a system that profited from the liquidations.”

JUST IN: ARTHUR HAYS AND PARTNERS STAND TO PROFIT $270 MILLION💰

BitMEX exchange is closing shop after 11 years, leaving its 'insurance fund' behind.

Hell of a paycheck for a defunct exchange 👀 pic.twitter.com/xF7esmc54l

— Bitcoin Archive (@BitcoinArchive) July 23, 2026

Did Server Outages Play A Role

Central to the case is an accusation that BitMEX's own internal trading desk had access to private customer data and kept trading even when the platform froze for everyone else.

Ordinary users, the complaint says, were locked out and unable to close or adjust positions during these outages, while the exchange's internal operation carried on regardless.

The plaintiffs are asking the court to order the return of the Bitcoin they say was wrongly withheld, plus compensatory and punitive damages.

The proposed class covers any US customer who traded Bitcoin perpetual swap products going back to 23 July 2018.

Is This The First Time BitMEX Has Faced These Allegations

A similar case was brought in 2020 by trader Brett Messieh and others under the Commodity Exchange Act, raising near identical claims about the exchange's liquidation practices.

That lawsuit was voluntarily dismissed without prejudice on 30 June 2025, which left the door open for the same allegations to resurface, as they now have.

Why Is BitMEX Closing Down

Hours before the lawsuit was filed, BitMEX's owner, HDR Global Trading, announced that the exchange would wind down entirely following a strategic review of the business and the wider digital asset market.

Trading will stop at 04:00 UTC on 23 September.

New account registrations have already been suspended, and from 26 August, existing users will only be able to reduce their positions rather than open new ones.

Any positions still open at the final cut off will be liquidated automatically.

BitMEX said reserves currently exceed customer liabilities and pointed to its published proof of reserves data as evidence that client funds remain fully backed.

Account access for withdrawals will continue after trading ends, though customers who leave funds on the platform will be charged $50 a month or 1% annually, whichever is greater.

The company also warned users to watch out for phishing attempts exploiting the shutdown news, stressing that no priority withdrawal service exists.

BitMEX is shutting down the exchange.

Primary source (BitMEX blog, 23 Jul 2026):
• Closure: 23 Sep 2026 04:00 UTC
• New signups already stopped
• From 26 Aug: no new positions; force-close path
• Post-close fee if funds remain (greater of ~$50/mo or 1%/yr)
• Phishing… pic.twitter.com/VWsY85CTMD

— riddik (@nvee3) July 23, 2026

How Has The Market Reacted

The shutdown news triggered an immediate sell off in BMEX, the exchange's native utility token, which dropped by around 90% following the announcement.

What's Behind The Leadership Shake Up

The closure caps months of upheaval inside the company.

Earlier this month, chief executive Stephan Lutz was replaced as part of a broader management overhaul that also saw chief financial officer Ina Steiner and chief growth officer Raphael Polansky depart.

Former chief operating officer and global general counsel Peter Wilkinson has since stepped in as CEO.

BitMEX has cycled through several leadership changes since 2020, when founders Arthur Hayes, Ben Delo and Samuel Reed left after US authorities accused the exchange of failing to enforce adequate anti money laundering checks, charges BitMEX later pleaded guilty to.

Alexander Höptner took over as CEO in 2021, followed by Lutz during the 2022 crypto market downturn.

Founded in 2014, BitMEX was among the first crypto derivatives exchanges and popularised the 100x leveraged perpetual swap, a product that later spread across the wider industry.

In its closure statement, the company said it had run for more than 11 years without losing customer funds to a hack, and thanked its user base for their support throughout that period.

data.title: The U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission (SEC) will hold a roundtable discussion on 24-hour trading of U.S. stocks.
data.description:
Source: US SEC; Compiled by: Jinse Finance Claw

>

The US SEC announced today that it will hold a roundtable meeting on September 17, 2026 to discuss the transition of the US stock market to 24-hour trading, including preparations to support overnight trading, the operation and resilience of the 24-hour market, and the opportunities and challenges of expansion.

SEC Chairman Paul S. Atkins stated, “We are moving towards a new 24-hour trading model for the U.S. stock market. With the expansion of overnight trading, I am pleased to see the U.S. stock market aligning with those markets that already offer 24-hour trading, and I look forward to achieving a balance between protecting investors and clients and 24-hour trading.”

The roundtable will be open to the public at the SEC headquarters at 100 F Street, Northeast Washington, D.C.

The discussion will be streamed live on SEC.gov, with recordings available later.

The roundtable agenda and speaker information will be announced prior to the meeting.

Please note that attendance may be limited, and all attendees will be subject to security checks.

The public wishing to comment on 24-hour trading may submit comments electronically or in paper form. Please use only one method. Submitted information will become a public record of the roundtable and will be published on the SEC website. All comments received will be published verbatim.

Please note that personally identifiable information will not be deleted or edited from submitted comments. You should only submit information you wish to make public. All submissions should include the file number 4-913. If submitting via email, please include the file number in the email subject line.

data.title: Andrew Tate's DADDY memecoin Plunges 97% As Influencer Continues To Tweets Behind Bars
data.description:
Andrew Tate's Solana-based memecoin DADDY has fallen 97% from its 2024 peak as the controversial influencer remains in federal custody in Miami, where he continues posting on social media while awaiting extradition proceedings to the United Kingdom.

The token, which Tate adopted as his flagship memecoin during the celebrity token frenzy of 2024, has become another casualty of the boom-and-bust cycle that swept through Solana's memecoin market.

DADDY has lost more than a quarter of its value since Tate's latest arrest and roughly half its value over the past week, leaving its market capitalization below $5 million.

Arrest adds to mounting legal troubles

U.S. Marshals arrested Andrew Tate, 39, and his brother Tristan, 38, outside Miami's James L. Knight Center on Saturday under a sealed warrant linked to UK extradition proceedings. The brothers were reportedly on their way to a boxing event that Andrew Tate was scheduled to co-host before being transferred to the Federal Detention Center in downtown Miami.

Both Andrew and Tristan Tate have been arrested by U.S. Marshals outside of a private event in Miami, Florida, following an extradition request by the UK Government against the brothers on over fifty charges related to rape, arranging or facilitating trafficking for sexual… pic.twitter.com/zW9qOOhlFU

— OSINTdefender (@sentdefender) July 19, 2026

Despite his detention, Tate has remained active on X, claiming he is being held in the prison's Special Housing Unit while describing harsh conditions, including being housed next to an alleged cannibal who "screams through the night." He also claimed to have no contact with the outside world, even as his posts continued attracting millions of views.

I am being held in SHU, the highest level of security which exists.

No commissary.
No visits.
No contact with the outside world.

My neighbor is a cannibal who screams throughout the night.

— Andrew Tate (@Cobratate) July 23, 2026

The arrests come after Britain's Crown Prosecution Service authorized 38 additional charges against the Tate brothers involving four more alleged victims, bringing the total number of alleged victims to seven.

Andrew Tate faces additional charges including rape, trafficking, assault, offenses relating to child sexual abuse images and extreme pornography, while Tristan Tate faces additional rape, sexual assault and trafficking charges. Their next court hearing is scheduled for July 27.

🚨 Andrew Tate is now facing 42 criminal charges. These include:
– 19 child p*rn charges
– 17 r*pe and sexual assault charges
– 3 human trafficking charges
– 3 assault charges pic.twitter.com/lmMGJ4VeXf

— GOP Ls (@GOP__Ls) July 21, 2026

Celebrity memecoin continues to unravel

DADDY, launched during the celebrity memecoin craze that swept crypto markets in 2024, now trades around $0.008 after peaking near $0.29.

The token's decline follows earlier concerns surrounding its distribution. Blockchain analytics platform Bubblemaps previously reported that the token's deployer transferred approximately 40% of the total supply directly to a wallet associated with Tate, raising questions about centralization and insider control.

DADDY's collapse also mirrors the fate of many celebrity-backed memecoins that emerged during the 2024 speculative boom. The Tate brothers endorsed more than 10 Solana memecoins during that period, most of which have since lost roughly 99% of their value.

Other Tate-linked tokens have performed even worse. TOPG, another Tate-themed memecoin that may not have been officially affiliated with him, now trades approximately 99.8% below its June 2024 peak on less than $200 in daily trading volume, while Tate Terminal has fallen more than 99% from its October 2024 high.

The Tate brothers are in solitary confinement in federal detention center of Miami, right behind me!

Behind these very walls!

I have spent $50,000 on a trip from Dubai to Miami just to make this video! The least you can do is watch this video till the end, understand whats… pic.twitter.com/ZI6up7WG7B

— Thomas Kralow (@TKralow) July 21, 2026

The latest decline reinforces the broader collapse of the celebrity memecoin phenomenon, where social media influence and controversy often fueled rapid rallies but rarely translated into lasting value once public attention shifted or legal problems overshadowed the personalities behind the tokens.

data.title: Goldman Sachs sets a price target of $640 for AMD: Microsoft and others begin deploying Helios.
data.description:
Author: BlockBeats

Following its San Francisco event on July 23, AMD received a reiterated buy rating from Goldman Sachs, with a 12-month price target of $640. The core reason given was that the company's collaborations with two major clients, Anthropic and Microsoft, and the projected $2 trillion total computing market by 2030, reinforced its growth story in AI infrastructure.

This wasn't just a simple GPU specifications release. AMD is attempting to shift the AI ​​competition from single chips to a delivery model encompassing "rack systems + CPU + network + DPU + software tools + large-customer deployments." For investors, the key question has shifted from "Does AMD have a stronger GPU?" to "Can it secure sufficiently large AI cluster orders and deliver on time?"

The report's price target of $640 is based on a P/E ratio of 32 and a normalized earnings per share estimate of $20.

Based on the current share price of approximately $539.69, the potential upside is about 18.6%; the company's market capitalization is approximately $890.5 billion. Revenue is projected to increase from approximately $34.6 billion to $109 billion between 2025 and 2028, with EPS increasing from $2.64 to $17.90, and a target price of $640. Anthropic has secured 2GW of orders, and Microsoft will begin receiving Helios systems from the second half of 2026. The most direct order signal comes from Anthropic. AMD and Anthropic have entered into a strategic partnership, with Anthropic deploying a total of 2GW of Instinct M1450 GPUs in its Helios rack-mount AI systems. The first 1GW is expected to be deployed starting in the first half of 2027. AMD also plans to provide Anthropic with up to $5 billion in strategic equity investment, and the two companies will jointly optimize the performance of Claude models on AMD GPUs and accelerate ROCm software development. The significance of such collaborations extends beyond chip sales. For AI chip suppliers, the deployment by leading model companies determines the credibility of the ecosystem. If Anthropic continues to migrate or expand Claude workloads to the AMD platform, it will help AMD prove that its GPUs, rack systems, and software stack can handle large-scale training and inference needs. Microsoft, on the other hand, offers another path to implementation. Following the expansion of their Azure partnership, Microsoft plans to begin receiving Helios racks, Venice CPUs, networking equipment, and software starting in the second half of 2026 for use in cutting-edge model inference, Microsoft's own AI services, and customer applications. Microsoft will also launch two new virtual machines based on the next-generation 2nm Venice CPU and expand the deployment of Pensando DPUs in its networking services. This means that AMD aims to sell GPUs, CPUs, DPUs, and networking equipment simultaneously within the cloud vendor ecosystem, rather than simply appearing as an accelerator card supplier. For Azure, if the AMD platform can provide sufficient performance and supply elasticity, it will also help reduce the pressure of relying on a single supply chain.

$2 Trillion TAM: Computing Power Amplification from Agent-Based AI

The most impactful figure in the report is AMD's upward revision of its 2030 total computing market size forecast to $2 trillion.

Of this, data center AI accelerator TAM has been revised upward from $200 billion to $1.4 trillion, corresponding to a 40% CAGR; server CPU TAM has been revised upward from $26 billion to $220 billion, corresponding to a 50% CAGR. AMD also predicts that by 2030, the company will hold a 50% share of the data center CPU market.

This assumption is underpinned by agent-based AI. Compared to single-question-answer sessions, agent-based AI requires calling tools, planning tasks, reading context, executing multi-step workflows, and handling more inference and orchestration requests. The computational demands don't just fall on GPUs; CPUs also handle scheduling, data preprocessing, system services, and multi-agent workflow orchestration. This is also the new story AMD wants to tell: the expansion of AI infrastructure not only drives demand for accelerators but also for server CPUs, networks, and system-level solutions. If companies can package EPYC CPUs, Instinct GPUs, Pensando DPUs, and network equipment into Helios racks, the revenue potential will be greater than from single-chip sales. However, this $2 trillion figure is not a realized market size but rather a prediction based on the large-scale adoption of agent-based AI by 2030. It requires enterprises and cloud vendors to continuously expand AI inference deployments, and also requires AI applications to truly move from pilot projects to high-frequency production workloads. Accelerator TAM has been increased from $200 billion to $1.4 trillion, CPU TAM from $26 billion to $220 billion, and total computing TAM to $2 trillion. Helios enters mass production; AMD aims to prove its rack-level delivery capabilities. At the product level, Helios is the core of AMD's campaign. The next-generation Helios AI rack platform is based on the CDNA 5 architecture, with single GPU performance reaching up to 40 PFLOPS (FP4) and 20 PFLOPS (FP8), equipped with 432GB of HBM4 memory and 23.3TB/s memory bandwidth. Each rack integrates 75 GPUs connected via UALink over Ethernet, along with a 96-core EPYC CPU, a Salina DPU, and a Volcano 800G AI network card. The focus of these parameters is not on single-point performance, but rather on AMD's push towards a rack-wide form factor. AI clusters increasingly rely on system-level design: GPU interconnects, memory bandwidth, network throughput, CPU scheduling capabilities, and software stacks all impact final training and inference efficiency. Helios is now in full production, with shipments planned to begin at the end of Q3 and mass production in Q4. Microsoft will receive Helios in the second half of 2026, and the first 1GW deployment of Anthropic will begin in the first half of 2027, meaning that true large-scale customer validation is still to come. AMD has also partnered with Cerebras to combine the Helios system with Cerebras' wafer-level engine to create a high-performance AI inference solution, aiming to achieve lower latency, higher energy efficiency, and up to 5x more tokens per watt. This solution is expected to launch through Cerebras Cloud in the second half of 2026. On the software side, the ROCm.ai platform has been officially launched, integrating AI tools such as Cursor, Claude, Codex, and Gemini, providing developers with an AI-driven software development experience. The accompanying Hyperloom optimization layer has optimized over 14,000 models, achieving an average performance improvement of 3.3x compared to ROCm 7. However, the collaboration between ROCm.ai and Cerebras is more suitable as a supplement to the Helios ecosystem than the main focus of this article. Ultimately, investors will look at whether customers can stably use the AMD platform under real-world loads, not just whether the tools and the list of partners are long enough. Valuation bets have been raised, with risks lying in deliveries in 2026-2027. Goldman Sachs' financial forecasts show that AMD's revenue is expected to be approximately $50.57 billion in 2026, with EPS of $6.20; approximately $86.01 billion in 2027, with EPS of $13.20; and approximately $109 billion in 2028, with EPS of $17.90. These forecasts indicate that the market has high expectations for AMD's AI revenue ramp-up, profit margin improvement, and operational leverage release over the next two to three years. The $640 target price reflects not only the current product launch but also the simultaneous progress of multiple aspects, including Helios shipments, Microsoft Azure deployments, the initial rollout of Anthropic's 1GW, and improvements to the ROCm ecosystem. The real disagreement lies here. First, the adoption of proxy AI may be slower than expected. If enterprise AI workflows do not expand rapidly, the assumption of a $2 trillion total computing market by 2030 will be revised downwards. Second, there is still a time lag in large customer GPU deployments. Microsoft's Helios acceptance will begin in the second half of 2026, and the initial 1GW of Anthropic is expected to begin in the first half of 2027; short-term financial reports cannot fully validate the revenue contribution of these collaborations. Third, competitive pressure will not disappear. Nvidia still dominates the AI ​​accelerator and software ecosystem. Whether ROCm can narrow the developer experience gap will affect AMD's substitutability among large-scale customers. Fourth, the x86 architecture also faces market share risks in enterprise AI scenarios. If customers increasingly adopt custom chips, Arm servers, or other heterogeneous solutions, AMD's expectations for CPU TAM and a 50% data center CPU market share will be challenged. This makes AMD's story more like an execution test: the report has already pushed market space, customer orders, and target prices to higher levels. Whether the stock price can continue to digest these figures depends on whether Helios can ship on schedule, whether Microsoft and Anthropic can expand deployment as planned, and whether AI demand can truly support a $2 trillion market by 2030.

data.title: Winklevoss Twins Donate $10 Million In Bitcoin Through Gemini To MAGA PAC During Ongoing CFTC Legal Battle
data.description:
Gemini Sends $10 Million In Bitcoin To Pro Trump PAC As CFTC Settlement Reversal Awaits

Gemini has contributed roughly $10 million in Bitcoin (BTC) to MAGA Inc., the super political action committee backing US President Donald Trump, placing the cryptocurrency exchange back in the spotlight as it continues to await a court decision over its regulatory dispute with the US Commodity Futures Trading Commission (CFTC).

Federal Election Commission filings show that Gemini Trust Company, led by co-founders Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss, made two separate Bitcoin donations worth more than $5 million each on 19 June 2026.

Gemini Sends $10M In Bitcoin To Trump Super PAC

Gemini donated $10 million in Bitcoin $BTC to the MAGA Inc. Super PAC weeks after seeking to reverse a $5 million CFTC settlement.

Federal Election Commission filings show the exchange made two separate Bitcoin contributions on… pic.twitter.com/Y2rca2Yzay

— BSCN (@BSCNews) July 24, 2026

The cryptocurrency was later sold through Gemini's own trading platform and converted into cash for the PAC.

The contribution is among the largest cryptocurrency donations disclosed during the current election cycle and adds to the growing use of digital assets in US political fundraising.

Court Has Yet To Rule On Gemini CFTC Settlement Request

The timing has drawn particular attention because the donation came around three weeks after Gemini and the CFTC jointly asked a federal court to reverse a January 2025 settlement involving the exchange.

The original case centred on allegations that Gemini made false or misleading statements.

A timeline:
-CFTC sued Gemini for lawbreaking
-President Trump was sworn in
-Gemini's Winklevoss twins reportedly sank the nomination of @BrianQuintenz to lead the CFTC
-The current Chairman was swapped in as the nominee and confirmed
-CFTC joins Gemini to avoid judgment pic.twitter.com/RUEIcmDiy2

— Tyler Gellasch (@TylerGellasch) May 28, 2026

Although both parties have requested that the settlement be vacated, a CFTC spokesperson previously said the agency and Gemini agreed that the original $5 million penalty would not be returned to the company if the court approves the motion.

The US District Court for the Southern District of New York has not yet issued a public decision on the request.

Current CFTC Chair Michael Selig has argued that the agency under former President Joe Biden had "politically targeted" the Winklevoss brothers through enforcement actions.

CFTC admits mistake: Joins Gemini in motion to vacate consent order from 2022 case.

“Complaint should not have been filed” under today’s standards.
Huge regulatory U-turn in crypto enforcement.https://t.co/RkDbMiVJPzhttps://t.co/nffOOok70Gpic.twitter.com/QtN4TZCkGN

— 𝗕𝗮𝗻𝗸XRP (@BankXRP) May 28, 2026

Political Ties Between The Winklevoss Twins And Trump Continue To Grow

The latest contribution builds on a series of high-profile donations and public support from the Winklevoss brothers for Trump and his administration.

During the 2024 presidential campaign, Cameron and Tyler Winklevoss each donated $1 million to Trump's campaign.

The Winklevoss twins, the founders of Gemini crypto exchange have donated 30.94 #bitcoin worth $2 million to Donald Trump to “end Biden Administration's war on crypto."

If you bet against crypto, you lose. pic.twitter.com/vQ4gorxeZn

— Layah Heilpern (@LayahHeilpern) June 20, 2024

After Trump returned to office in January 2025, the brothers attended the signing ceremony for the GENIUS Act, a stablecoin payments law, publicly supported his sons' crypto mining venture American Bitcoin, and later contributed $21 million in Bitcoin to the Digital Freedom Fund PAC to back the administration's cryptocurrency agenda.

Today, @cameron and I donated $21 million in bitcoin (188.4547 BTC) to the Digital Freedom Fund PAC. The mission of the @FreedomFundPAC is to help realize President Trump’s vision of making America the crypto capital of the world. Since inauguration, @POTUS and his Administration…

— Tyler Winklevoss (@tyler) August 20, 2025

The twins have also been frequent visitors to the White House since Trump's return to office.

Critics Raise Questions Over Timing And Political Influence

The donation has prompted renewed debate over whether large cryptocurrency contributions could create the appearance of political influence, particularly while Gemini remains involved in an unresolved regulatory matter.

Michael Beckel, Money in Politics Reform director at campaign finance watchdog Issue One, criticised the contribution, saying,

"Nothing says thank you after a favorable regulatory or enforcement action like massive super PAC contributions."

Nothing says thank you after a favorable regulatory or enforcement action like massive super PAC contributions.https://t.co/5Pww2OVTMNhttps://t.co/92YQP5ROAD

— Michael Beckel (@mjbeckel) July 21, 2026

He added,

"It's a threat to our democracy when wealthy megadonors and special interests can buy access and influence, get favorable results, and then invest even larger sums hoping for even more favorable treatment. Showering politicians with campaign cash after favorable treatment perpetuates a cycle of cynicism."

Senator Elizabeth Warren has also questioned the CFTC's handling of the Gemini case.

In a letter sent to Selig in June 2026, she described the attempt to reverse the settlement and related developments as "concerning signs of a CFTC beholden to political pressures and interests of the wealthy insiders, unbound by the rule of law and failing to protect investors and market integrity."

Warren also referenced reports that former CFTC commissioner Brian Quintenz's nomination to lead the agency was withdrawn after he allegedly refused requests from Tyler Winklevoss to prioritise overturning the enforcement action.

Senator Elizabeth Warren Questions CFTC Chair on Agency’s Ability to Regulate Prediction Markets After Report of Trump Corruption and Industry Capture

Letter: https://t.co/0Xgsa4Wqf9

Press Release: https://t.co/2SA6kUahQ5pic.twitter.com/VI1pcLu7c1

— Daniel Wallach (@WALLACHLEGAL) June 9, 2026

According to Warren's letter, Tyler Winklevoss reportedly told Quintenz he would be "happy to raise this issue with the president himself."

Crypto Donations Gain Ground As Congress Debates New Rules

The Gemini contribution comes as Congress continues debating the Digital Asset Market Clarity (CLARITY) Act, legislation expected to hand the CFTC broader authority over digital asset markets.

Selig currently remains the agency's only commissioner, despite the CFTC traditionally operating with five bipartisan commissioners.

Lawmakers have continued pressing the Trump administration to nominate additional members as the regulator prepares for a potentially larger role in overseeing the cryptocurrency industry.

Campaign finance filings also show MAGA Inc. had received more than $397 million by 30 June 2026, highlighting the scale of fundraising behind the committee supporting Trump's political agenda.

data.title: Why are Southeast Asian crypto fraud networks, which cause annual losses exceeding $100 billion, getting richer despite crackdowns?
data.description:
Author: ChandlerZ, Foresight News

On July 21, the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC) released its "Transnational Organized Crime Threat Assessment 2026" (TOCTA 2026) report in Bangkok. The report estimates that losses from cyber fraud in East Asia, Southeast Asia, Australia, and New Zealand will be between US$88.3 billion and US$114.1 billion in 2025, approximately three times the 2023 estimate of US$18 billion to US$37 billion.

The report lists cryptocurrency as a key financial infrastructure in this criminal economy, noting that USDT on the TRON chain has become the preferred money laundering tool for fraud groups, and a single crypto service provider (VASP) located in the Mekong River region processed between US$49 billion and US$64 billion in crypto transactions between 2021 and 2024.

The upper limit of losses, $114.1 billion, exceeds the GDP of several Southeast Asian countries. Even using the lower limit of $88.3 billion, fraud losses in 2025 would still be 2.4 times the peak estimate in 2023. Geographically, East Asia bears approximately 71% of the losses, Australia and New Zealand account for 15.8% to 20.2%, and Southeast Asia itself accounts for only 8.4% to 13.2%. Southeast Asia is a breeding ground for criminal activity, with victims distributed throughout the Asia-Pacific region and even globally. A separate assessment by the U.S. Treasury Department indicates that Americans alone lost over $10 billion to fraud in Southeast Asia in 2024. The number of reported cases in various countries is also rising sharply; in Thailand, cybercrime cases increased from less than 20,000 in 2023 to over 380,000 in 2025, with cumulative losses exceeding $700 million. In Malaysia, online fraud losses reached approximately US$730 million in 2025, more than doubling year-on-year, with the number of cases surging 87% to over 66,000. In Singapore, fraud losses reached US$822 million in 2024, a 70.6% year-on-year increase, before decreasing to US$686 million in 2025 after legislative intervention. [Image of a map showing fraud zones in Cambodia, Myanmar, and Laos] Cryptocurrency as a Financial Conduit for the Criminal Economy: USDT on the TRON blockchain, due to its fast transaction speed, low fees, and high anonymity, has become the preferred channel for fraud groups to process and transfer funds. Chainalysis data shows that by 2025, stablecoins accounted for 84% of the total global illicit crypto transactions. A single crypto service provider located in the Mekong River basin processed between $49 billion and $64 billion in crypto transactions between 2021 and 2024, making it the largest of its kind in the Asia-Pacific region. Beyond escrow platforms, decentralized networks known as CMLNs (Chinese Money Laundering Networks) are also rapidly expanding. Chainalysis data shows that in 2025, CMLNs processed $16.1 billion in illicit crypto assets through 1,799 active wallets, using cross-chain bridges, DeFi protocols, and mixers for layered money laundering. DeFi money laundering kits priced at only a few hundred dollars have already appeared on the market, capable of automating fund splitting, cross-chain transfers, and obfuscation operations.

Player → Local Bank → Mule Account → USDT on TRON → Offshore Wallet Money Laundering Path

Industrialized Operation and Global Expansion

These criminal networks have become highly industrialized. Delphine Schantz, UNODC Southeast Asia and Pacific Representative, describes their operating model as corporate franchising, with each link handled by a specialized team: money laundering, human trafficking, smuggling, and data theft, all sharing the same financial and logistical infrastructure.

The report categorizes criminal organizations into four tiers: a strategic leadership layer with overseas managers and asset allocation; an operational management layer responsible for regional coordination and corrupt relationships; an operational layer comprised of recruiters, technicians, and coerced laborers; and a network of accomplices including lawyers, underground bank operators, cryptocurrency exchange dealers, and corrupt officials. At least 300,000 people are trapped in scam parks across Southeast Asia, with approximately 6 million more involved in a broader crime-supporting ecosystem. Victims from at least 80 countries and regions are lured to these parks by fake job advertisements, then detained and forced to engage in online scams. Data from the Thai Anti-Fraud Center in 2025 shows that the country identified 13,195 individuals from 83 countries through its national referral mechanism, of whom 4,407 were confirmed as victims of human trafficking. Conditions in these parks are appalling; newcomers have their passports and mobile phones confiscated, undergo medical examinations such as blood tests, and are then taken to dormitories protected by barbed wire and military guard posts. Whistleblowers at the K99 Triumph City campus reported being beaten and electrocuted. Recruitment networks have extended to transit hubs in Asia, the Middle East, and Africa, with advertisements targeting job seekers in Europe and North America, specifically seeking speakers of German, French, Spanish, Italian, Dutch, Polish, Swedish, Norwegian, and English. Criminal networks are also expanding into South America and Africa, with Asian criminal groups establishing physical fraud centers in South America, recruiting Spanish and Portuguese speakers, and forging partnerships with major local drug cartels. Deepfake Exploitation Chain: Inputs → AI Tools → Outputs → Impacts On the technical level, criminal groups are deploying AI deepfake and automated fraud systems. Malicious advertising incidents increased by 42% year-on-year in 2025, with criminals using legitimate advertising networks to distribute malware to large numbers of users. UN News previously reported that deepfakes and voice cloning technologies have become weaponized tools in organized fraud. Criminal groups also utilize Starlink's portability and independence from national telecommunications networks to maintain fraud operations in remote areas such as Myanmar and Laos, ensuring their operations are not interrupted even when local governments cut off traditional internet access. The Sulu-Sulawesi sea, a smuggling route connecting Indonesia, Malaysia, and the Philippines, is also used to transport technical equipment supporting fraud operations, including low-Earth orbit satellite devices. ClickFix social engineering attacks surged by 517% in the first half of 2025. An affiliate of the Lazarus Group adapted this technique into a variant called ClickFake Interview, specifically targeting cryptocurrency industry professionals by forging recruitment processes. 48% of cybersecurity professionals listed Agentic AI (AI systems capable of autonomously planning, executing, and adjusting multi-step attacks) as the largest attack vector in 2026. The number of AI service providers in the escrow market grew at an average annual rate of 1,900% between 2021 and 2024, receiving $375.9 million in cryptocurrency transactions in 2024. Combat Actions and Law Enforcement Bottlenecks Many countries are intensifying their efforts to combat these attacks. The U.S. Department of Justice's Scam Center Strike Force seized $580 million in cryptocurrency within its first three months. Cambodia arrested Chen Zhi, a key figure in a fraud network, and completed one of the largest Bitcoin seizures in the country's history. Thailand announced a deepened cooperation with the US FBI in combating global fraud networks on the same day the report was released. In April 2026, the US Treasury Department's OFAC sanctioned Cambodian Senator Kok An and his K99 Group, along with Rithy Raksmei and 26 other individuals and entities, for using forced labor by human trafficking victims in fraudulent schemes to carry out digital asset investment fraud and romance scams. Thailand's Anti-Money Laundering Office (AMLO) and civil courts had previously issued temporary freeze orders on assets associated with Kok An, involving assets valued at 13.07 billion baht (approximately US$407 million). In February 2025, the North Korean-affiliated Lazarus Group stole approximately US$1.5 billion worth of ETH from the cryptocurrency exchange Bybit and laundered the money through the THORChain cross-chain protocol. Wallet drainer attacks stole $494 million in 2024, a 67% year-on-year increase, targeting over 300,000 wallets. While targeted operations in Myanmar and Laos destroyed some fraud rings, criminal networks are rapidly migrating to areas with weaker law enforcement. UNODC Chief Analyst Inshik Sim warns that the scale and complexity of the criminal economy are exceeding the design limits of existing response systems. The ability of criminal organizations to create self-sustaining stablecoins, self-built blockchains, and encrypted communication platforms means that the current primary means of financial intervention—the asset freeze—may be ineffective. Delphine Schantz argues that law enforcement agencies need to shift from targeting individual criminal acts to systematically tracking and seizing criminal proceeds, while extending regulatory frameworks to the underlying blockchain infrastructure level, rather than relying solely on cooperation with individual stablecoin issuers.

data.title: CICC: The Financial Moment of AI
data.description:
Authors: Lin Yingqi, Che Shuyun, et al.; Source: CICC Insights

AI capital expenditure is at a turning point, shifting from being driven by internal cash flow to expanding the balance sheet. As investment scale exceeds the carrying capacity of internal cash flow, marginal funding will increasingly come from the financial system. This report, the first in the "AI Financing Tracking" series, attempts to answer three questions: How will the financial system absorb the $3.5 trillion AI financing demand? Can AI investment meet debt repayment and equity returns? What opportunities will this round of AI financing cycle bring to financial institutions, and what risks will it accumulate and transmit?

Abstract

AI infrastructure capital expenditure: From cash flow to debt. The ratio of capital expenditure to revenue for large cloud vendors rose from approximately 12% in 2023 to approximately 23% in 2025. According to market consensus expectations, this ratio may further rise to over 40% in 2027, approaching or even exceeding the historical highs of capital expenditure cycles in the internet and energy industries.

Under pressure from AI capital expenditures, cloud vendors' AI investments may shift from operating cash flow to greater reliance on external financing. Based on market consensus expectations for operating cash flow and capital expenditures of major cloud vendors, we estimate that AI capital expenditures are expected to generate approximately $3.5 trillion in external financing needs over the next five years. Where will this $3.5 trillion come from? In the baseline scenario, we expect that the $3.5 trillion in external financing for AI capital expenditures will be absorbed by public market equity ($0.4 trillion), investment-grade bonds ($1.5 trillion), leveraged financing ($0.3 trillion), asset-backed securities ($0.3 trillion), and private equity ($1.1 trillion). Investment-grade bonds and private equity are the ballast for financing; the former relies on the expansion of cloud vendors' balance sheets and their cash flow repayment capabilities, while the latter can meet the financing needs of higher-risk, larger-scale projects and can also reduce cloud vendors' capital investment through off-balance-sheet financing structures, allowing for more flexible supplementation of financing gaps. A trillion-dollar question: How will AI debt be repaid? To meet debt repayment needs and shareholder returns, assuming a ROIC of 10%, we estimate that AI applications will ultimately need to generate approximately $1 trillion in sustainable revenue annually. Assuming this revenue scale is reached by 2030, this means AI application revenue needs to nearly double annually over the next five years. More important than revenue scale are profit margins and asset lifespan: at a mature EBITDA profit margin of approximately 50%, infrastructure depreciation periods of approximately 5 years are needed for investments to potentially yield positive returns; if the profit margin is below 20%, even with a longer asset lifespan, it will be difficult to cover capital costs. Can refinancing replace cash flow? Large cloud vendors typically issue long-term bonds with maturities of 10-30 years, and infrastructure funds often have investment periods of 10 years or more; after data centers are operational, construction period financing can be replaced through project bonds and securitization. However, refinancing can only buy time, not replace cash flow: if project utilization, profit margins, and asset lifespan are consistently lower than expected, continuous rolling financing will only increase leverage and financing costs. Our calculations indicate that the peak maturity period for bonds issued by large cloud vendors will be from 2027 to 2032, with an annual maturity scale of approximately $28 billion, a 60% increase compared to 2024-2026, leading to increased refinancing pressure. Opportunities and Risks for Financial Institutions. Banks can generate revenue from equity and debt underwriting, trading, M&A advisory, and project financing, while private equity and insurance institutions gain new long-term assets. In the second quarter of 2026, the non-interest income of the six largest US banks increased by 32% year-on-year; as of June 2026, bank loans to non-bank financial institutions increased by approximately 25% year-on-year, with AI financing being a significant contributor to this growth. Bank revenue is typically recognized during the financing and construction phases, while credit risk only becomes apparent during project commissioning and refinancing, exhibiting a "revenue upfront, risk down" characteristic. Is AI a Financial "Bubble"? Currently, AI investment is primarily driven by large cloud vendors with strong cash flow, and equity and subordinated capital can absorb losses before the senior tranche of bank loans. Therefore, even if some projects fail to meet expectations, the risk is more likely to manifest first as valuation adjustments for related companies, a slowdown in capital expenditure, and localized credit losses, rather than immediately escalating into a financial system crisis. On the other hand, the rapid growth of external financing, particularly private equity and leveraged financing, has also created financial spillover effects. If commercialization growth continues to lag behind capital expenditure, the risk will gradually shift from valuation corrections to credit quality deterioration, and will be transmitted through channels such as revolving financing, private lending, and securitization within the financial system. The scale of financing itself is not the problem; the real test is whether a cash flow cycle can be established before financing costs rise and assets depreciate.

Chart 1: Where did the $3.5 trillion in AI financing come from?

...

Note: AI capital expenditure and OCF support are based on market consensus expectations; 10% equity ratio in the public market is assumed; the scale of investment-grade bond issuance is calculated based on the debt ratings, leverage ratios, and bond concentration constraints of large cloud vendors; leveraged financing includes high-yield bonds and leveraged loans, and securitized assets include ABS and CMBS, estimated considering market capacity and acceptance; gaps other than the above financing methods are assumed to be covered by private capital, including infrastructure funds, private equity funds, private credit, real estate funds, etc.

Demonstration calculations, not actual predictions. Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. AI Infrastructure Capital Expenditure: From Cash Flow to Debt. A New Cycle of AI Capital Expenditure Creates Financing Demand. Market predictions for AI capital expenditure over the next five years are mostly between $4 trillion and $8 trillion. There are significant differences in the calculation methods and logic. We selected the market consensus expectation of approximately $5.6 trillion as the assumption for AI capital expenditure from 2026 to 2030 to provide a basis for subsequent calculations of the financing gap. Specifically, we used the capital expenditure of five major cloud vendors [1] in 2025 as the base [2] and used the market consensus expectation of AI-related revenue of NVIDIA and AMD [3] as a proxy indicator to deduce the future investment growth rate of the AI ​​industry chain. The market consensus forecasts a 5-year CAGR of approximately 44% for AI-related revenue for NVIDIA and AMD, significantly higher than the market consensus forecast of around 21% growth in capital expenditures supported by cloud vendors' operating cash flow by the end of 2025. This difference suggests that relying solely on cloud vendors' operating cash flow may be insufficient to support AI capital expenditures, necessitating greater external financing in the future. Chart 2: Chip manufacturers' AI-related revenue growth rate is higher than that of cloud vendors' capital expenditure growth rate. [Image of chart 2: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7493954_image3.png] Note: Data for 2026-2030 is based on Bloomberg consensus estimates; this is a demonstrative calculation and does not represent actual forecasts. Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department. Large cloud vendors: From cash flow to debt. As of the end of 2025, Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, Meta, and Oracle had combined assets of approximately $2.6 trillion, a debt-to-equity ratio of approximately 45%, and a net debt/EBITDA ratio of only about 0.3. These five companies collectively generated approximately $580 billion in operating cash flow annually. However, under pressure from AI capital expenditures, some cloud vendors have seen their leverage ratios rise. The ratio of capital expenditures to revenue for these five companies increased from approximately 12% in 2023 to approximately 23% in 2025, and according to market consensus, this ratio may further rise to over 40% by 2027, approaching or even exceeding the historical highs of capital expenditure cycles in the internet and energy industries. The market expects the combined free cash flow of major cloud vendors to decrease from approximately $200 billion in 2025 to approximately $24 billion in 2027, correspondingly reducing funds available for stock buybacks, dividends, and other purposes. Therefore, the AI ​​investments of major cloud vendors may shift from operating cash flow to greater reliance on external financing. Chart 3: AI vendors benefit from cloud vendors' capital expenditures, but declining free cash flow for cloud vendors puts pressure on stock prices. [Image of chart 3: https://img.jinse.com.cn/7493955_image3.png] Note: 2026-2027 forecasts use Bloomberg consensus estimates; the AI ​​vendor stock price index includes NVIDIA, AMD, Broadcom, SK Hynix, Samsung Electronics, Micron Technology, and ASML; the AI ​​cloud vendor stock price index includes Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, Alphabet, and Oracle.
Source: Factset, Bloomberg, Listed Company Announcements, CICC Research Department

Chart 4: Cloud Vendors' Capital Expenditure Intensity Rises, Expected to Surpass the Dot-com Bubble and Energy Bubble

Note: 2026-2028 uses Bloomberg consensus forecasts


Source: Factset, Bloomberg, Listed Company Announcements, CICC Research Department

$3.5 Trillion: The Financing Gap for AI Capital Expenditure

Chart 5: AI Capital Expenditure Creates a $3.5 Trillion Financing Gap

Note: This is a demonstrative calculation and does not represent actual predictions.

The scale of AI capital expenditure in the industry is calculated backwards from the AI-related revenue growth rates of Nvidia and AMD and the capital expenditure of major cloud vendors in 2025; capital expenditure supported by operating cash flow is estimated based on the Bloomberg consensus forecast of capital expenditure of major cloud vendors at the end of 2025, taking into account the capital expenditure space beyond the needs of dividends and share buybacks within operating cash flow; Source: Listed company announcements, Bloomberg, CICC Research Department Where will the $3.5 trillion come from: AI financing structure Investment-grade bonds: the ballast stone of AI financing We estimate that investment-grade bonds can provide approximately $1.5 trillion in funding for AI investment between 2026 and 2030, accounting for approximately 42% of external financing needs, making it the largest single financing channel. Major cloud vendors have strong operating cash flow, low initial leverage, and investment-grade credit ratings, enabling them to obtain large-scale, long-term, and relatively low-cost funding in the global bond market. The investment-grade bond market is capable of absorbing the majority of AI financing, but the continued large-scale, long-term bond supply may also increase the concentration of the technology industry in credit indices, leading to problems such as widening long-term credit spreads, rising financing costs, and strengthened rating constraints. Leveraged Financing: Transitional Capital for High-Growth Companies

Based on the market size ratio of leveraged financing and investment-grade bonds (approximately 20%), we estimate that high-yield bonds and leveraged loans could provide approximately $0.3 trillion in funding for AI investments, accounting for approximately 8% of external financing needs. This market primarily serves Neocloud, HPC operators, and data center companies that have not yet obtained investment-grade ratings but possess a certain revenue and asset base.

[Image of chart 9: Data as of July 14, 2026] [Source: Bloomberg, CICC Research Department] Public Market Equity: From “De-equityization” to “Re-equityization” Assuming a 20% equity ratio for projects (the industry average is typically 10%-30%), with 50% provided by public equity, we estimate that public equity could provide approximately $0.4 trillion in funding for AI investments between 2026 and 2030. Equity capital does not require fixed repayment of principal and interest, and can initially bear the risks of technological iteration, commercialization, and asset residual value. Therefore, it is more suitable for AI model companies, Neocloud, and data center operators that have not yet formed stable cash flow. Over the past decade, US-listed companies have tended to repurchase shares and increase dividends, thereby reducing outstanding shares and increasing earnings per share. This has weakened the equity market's function as a financing channel, a phenomenon known as "de-equityization." "Re-equityization" signifies a reversal of this trend, with companies relying more heavily on new issuances to raise funds. Equity financing is highly dependent on market valuation levels and issuance windows; once market volatility increases or valuations decline, the pace of financing may slow significantly. Securitization Products: From Corporate Credit to Asset Credit. Assuming that AI financing through securitization products will double in 26-30 years compared to 25-year levels, we estimate that ABS, CMBS, and other asset-backed financing could provide approximately $0.3 trillion in funding between 2026 and 2030, accounting for about 9% of external financing needs. Securitization financing is primarily applicable to data center and computing power assets that are already operational, have long-term leases, or identifiable equipment cash flows, shifting the financing basis from overall corporate credit to specific assets, leases, and cash flows. Securitization helps unlock construction loans and private equity capital, and provides long-term assets for insurance, pension, and fixed-income funds, but its expansion still depends on lease stability, tenant credit, asset residual value, and standardization. Especially for devices with rapid technological iterations, such as GPUs, the rate of depreciation and the value in the secondary market may become important constraints on rating and structural design. Private Equity: A Flexible Supplement to the Financing Gap

We estimate that private equity (including infrastructure funds, private equity, private credit, and real estate funds) can absorb approximately $1.1 trillion in funding, accounting for about 30% of external financing needs, second only to investment-grade bonds.

We estimate that private equity (including infrastructure funds, private equity, private credit, and real estate funds, etc.) can absorb approximately $1.1 trillion in funding, accounting for about 30% of external financing needs, second only to investment-grade bonds.

The advantage of private equity capital lies in its flexible structure, allowing for the combination of equity, preferred stock, mezzanine financing, and project loans to mitigate construction period and non-standardized risks that are difficult to price in the public market. Private equity funds can also establish project joint ventures (JVs) or special purpose vehicles (SPVs) with cloud vendors, introducing equity and debt financing at the project level. Subject to accounting conditions, such arrangements can create off-balance-sheet financing, reducing the cloud vendors' upfront capital investment and consolidation burden, while simultaneously diversifying risk to external capital. However, the supply of private equity capital is subject to certain liability-side constraints. According to data from Preqin and McKinsey, in the 12 months ending June 2025, the cash distribution ratio of private equity funds to AUM was approximately 6%, a recent low; redemption pressure on some private equity credit semi-liquid products also increased further in 2026. Therefore, private equity capital is expected to become an important source of incremental AI financing, but its actual absorption capacity still depends on fund raising and product liquidity.
